737	\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
738	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
739	
740	\begin{theorem}
741	
742	\leanok
743	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
744	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
746	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
747	
748	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
749	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
750	  \[
751	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
752	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
753	  \]
754	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
755	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
756	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
757	
758	  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
759	  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
760	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
761	  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
762	  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
763	  above.
764	\end{theorem}
765	
766	\begin{proof}
767	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
768	  \leanok
769	  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
770	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
771	  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
772	  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
773	  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
774	  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
775	  \[
776	    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
777	  \]
778	  Taking \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\) of the zero module gives
779	  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
780	\end{proof}

844	description \(\mathtt{skyscraperPresheaf}\). The chapter does not
845	introduce any new \texttt{import} beyond what
846	\texttt{RRFormula.lean} already pulls.
847	
848	\paragraph{Downstream consumers.} Two consumers depend on the chapter's
849	main output \cref{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}:
850	\begin{itemize}
851	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}'s parent lemma
852	    \texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf} consumes it to close
853	    the H\(^1\) half of \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\); this is the Lane H
854	    substrate input that the iter-188 strategy revision named.
855	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP} (the \texttt{RR.3} chapter) uses
856	    the same vanishing as part of its long-exact-sequence chain for the
857	    structural short exact sequence
858	    \(0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C([P]) \to k(P) \to 0\);
859	    combined with the genus-\(0\) input
860	    \(\dim H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) this delivers
861	    \(\dim H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), the non-constant-section
862	    output of \texttt{RR.3}.
863	\end{itemize}
864	
865	\section{Out of scope}
866	
867	The following items lie outside this chapter and are scheduled into
868	sibling sub-builds:
869	\begin{itemize}
870	  \item \emph{General coherent-sheaf cohomology vanishing.} Higher
871	    cohomology vanishing for general coherent sheaves on a curve
872	    requires Grothendieck vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme
873	    (Hartshorne~III.2.7), which the chapter does \emph{not} use; the
874	    chapter is narrowly tailored to the flasque/skyscraper case
875	    \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) that the genus-\(0\) critical path needs.
876	  \item \emph{Serre duality for curves.} The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch
877	    statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) routes through
878	    Serre duality \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
879	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*\); Mathlib has neither the dualizing sheaf
880	    \(\omega_C\) on a scheme nor the duality theorem. The chapter
881	    sidesteps duality entirely by using flasqueness directly (the
882	    Hartshorne~III~\S2 order of presentation, not the
883	    Hartshorne~III~\S7 one).
884	  \item \emph{The \(H^0\) half of \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).} The
885	    one-dimensionality identification \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) is the
886	    sibling substrate lemma
887	    REF of
888	    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, closed axiom-clean iter-188
889	    against the project's four-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain.
890	    The present chapter is the complementary H\(^1\) half.
891	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	% --- iter-187 plan-phase additions: pins for the iter-187 refactor's
102	% Step 3 + Step 4 sub-helpers (carrierPresheaf functor + sheaf property)
103	% which are the direct-construction substrate the definition below
104	% consumes (via the iter-186 plan-phase `analogies/ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
105	% Decision 3 + Decision 4 recipe). The Lean declarations are produced
106	% by the iter-187 refactor-`ocofp-steps3to5` dispatch. The substantive
107	% mathematical content is in the Definition block below; these helper
108	% blocks pin the substrate hooks the prover-side construction uses.

110	\begin{definition}
111	\leanok
112	[Presheaf carrier for the line bundle of a closed point]
113	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf}
115	  For each open \(U \subseteq C\), the \(\bar k\)-submodule
116	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \subset K(C)\) of section candidates
117	  satisfying the order conditions ``\(\mathrm{ord}_Q f \ge 0\) for all
118	  prime divisors \(Q \in U\) other than \(P\), and additionally
119	  \(\mathrm{ord}_P f \ge -1\) when \(P \in U\)'' assembles into a
120	  \(\bar k\)-linear functor
121	  \[
122	    \texttt{carrierPresheaf}_P : (\Opens\, |C|)^{\op}
123	    \longrightarrow \ModuleCat_{\bar k},
124	    \qquad U \longmapsto \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U),
125	  \]
126	  with restriction \(V \subseteq U \mapsto \texttt{Submodule.inclusion}\,
127	  (\texttt{carrierSet\_mono})\) on each pair. The functor laws follow from
128	  the antitone monotonicity \(U \mapsto \mathrm{carrierSet}_P(U)\) (witness
129	  \(\texttt{carrierSet\_mono}\) at L181-192 of the Lean file) by
130	  \(\texttt{ext}\,\langle x, \_\rangle\); \texttt{rfl}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{lemma}
134	\leanok
135	[Sheaf property of the carrier presheaf]
136	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
138	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf,
139	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
140	  The presheaf \texttt{carrierPresheaf}\(_P\) (defined via the
141	  carrier-refinement of \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf})
142	  is a sheaf in the appropriate Grothendieck topology on \(C\). Concretely,
143	  the locality + gluing conditions for \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued sections
144	  reduce (via \(\texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheaf\_forget}\)) to the sheaf
145	  condition on the underlying Set-valued functor: each
146	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U)\) is a subset of the constant
147	  \(K(C)\)-sheaf restricted to \(U\), and the sheaf property in the
148	  constant-sheaf base case is direct. The project's
149	  \texttt{toModuleKSheaf} template
150	  (\texttt{Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/SheafProperty.lean}) is
151	  the model for this argument.
152	\end{lemma}

154	\begin{definition}
155	
156	\leanok
157	[Carrier-submodule refinement enforcing \(F(\bot)=0\)]
158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
160	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
161	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
166	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
167	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.
168	  The refinement
169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
171	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,
172	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;
173	    \{\, f \;\mid\; U \ne \bot \,\lor\, f = 0 \,\}.
174	  \]
175	  forces \(F(\bot) = \bot\) (intersection annihilates) while preserving
176	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).
177	  The case-based restriction map (zero target on \(\bot\); inclusion
178	  otherwise) gives a well-formed sheaf-condition substrate consumed by
179	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}. Downstream
180	  consumers \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, \texttt{toFunctionField},
181	  \texttt{globalSections\_iff\_*} are updated to supply the
182	  \texttt{trivAtBot} witness at \(\op\,\top\) via the \texttt{htop\_ne\_bot}
183	  helper.
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	
189	\leanok
190	[Line bundle of a closed point]
191	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
193	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
204	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
208	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}
268	
269	\begin{definition}
270	
271	\leanok
272	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
273	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
275	  
276	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
277	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
278	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
279	  % of the construction) (read from
280	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
281	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
282	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
283	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
284	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
285	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
286	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
287	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
288	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
289	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
290	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
291	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
292	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
293	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
294	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
295	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
296	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
297	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)
298	  produces a canonical \(\bar k\)-linear map
299	  \[
300	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
301	      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
302	  \]
303	  sending a global section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to the rational
304	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of
305	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into \(\mathcal K_C\) at each point. The map \(\iota\) is
306	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
307	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections
308	  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme \(C\)).
309	
310	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
311	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
312	  takes the same inputs as REF (a curve
313	  \(C\), a point \(P\), and the closedness witness
314	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C.left)}) and produces, for each global
315	  section \(s\) of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) at twist \(0\) in the project's
316	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured global-sections pipeline, the corresponding
317	  element \(\iota(s) \in K(C)\) of the project's function-field carrier
318	  \texttt{C.left.functionField}. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}
319	  and its body is gated on the body of
320	  REF.
321	\end{definition}

323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	  
393	\leanok
394	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
395	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
396	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
397	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
398	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
399	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
400	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
401	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
402	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
403	  and only if:
404	
405	  \begin{itemize}
406	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
407	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
408	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
409	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
410	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
411	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
412	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
413	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
414	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
415	  \end{itemize}
416	  In divisorial language (REF,
417	  REF), the two conditions combine to
418	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
419	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
420	  zero global section.
421	
422	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
423	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
424	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
425	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
426	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
427	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
428	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
429	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
430	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
431	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
432	\end{proof}

434	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	
439	\leanok
440	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
441	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
443	  
444	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
445	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
446	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
447	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
448	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
449	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
450	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
451	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
452	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
453	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
454	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
455	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
456	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
457	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
458	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
459	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
460	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
461	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
462	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
463	  \[
464	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
465	  \]
466	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
467	\end{lemma}
468	
469	\begin{proof}
470	  
471	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
472	  exact sequence
473	  \[
474	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
475	  \]
476	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
477	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
478	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
479	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
480	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
481	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
482	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
483	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
484	  \[
485	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
486	  \]
487	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
488	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
489	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
490	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
491	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
492	  \begin{multline*}
493	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
494	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
495	  \end{multline*}
496	  Two ingredients close the proof:
497	
498	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
499	  hypothesis: by REF,
500	  \[
501	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
502	  \]
503	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
504	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
505	
506	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
507	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
508	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
509	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
510	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
511	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
512	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
513	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
514	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
515	
516	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
517	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
518	  \[
519	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
520	  \]
521	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
522	
523	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
524	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
525	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
526	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
527	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
528	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
529	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
530	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
531	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
532	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
533	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
534	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
535	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
536	  complex degenerates).
537	\end{proof}

539	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
540	
541	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The Lean body is now structurally
542	% sorry-free (closed transitively through two named substrate
543	% helpers). The substrate helpers `h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two`
544	% (the Int-valued $H^0 - H^1 = 2$ \(\chi\)-bridge; gated on
545	% `OcOfD.lean` STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED + `RRFormula.lean` $\chi$-additivity)
546	% and `h1_vanishing_genusZero` (gated on Lane H \texttt{H1Vanishing.lean}
547	% substrate) carry the remaining typed sorries.  Future consumers
548	% reading the body should consult those.
549	\begin{theorem}
550	
551	
552	\leanok
553	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
554	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
555	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
556	  
557	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
558	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
559	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
560	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
561	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
562	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
563	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
564	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
565	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
566	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
567	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
568	  % is the Euler characteristic
569	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
570	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
571	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
572	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
573	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
574	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
575	  \[
576	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
577	  \]
578	\end{theorem}
579	
580	\begin{proof}
581	  
582	  
583	  
584	\leanok
585	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
586	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
587	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
588	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
589	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
590	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
591	  \[
592	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
593	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
594	  \]
595	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
596	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
597	  \[
598	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
599	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
600	    \;=\; 2.
601	  \]
602	  By
603	  REF, the second
604	  term vanishes:
605	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
606	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
607	
608	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
609	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
610	  REF computes
611	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
612	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
613	  \[
614	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
615	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
616	  \]
617	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
618	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
619	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
620	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
621	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
622	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
623	  segment yields the short exact sequence
624	  \[
625	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
626	  \]
627	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
628	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
629	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
630	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
631	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
632	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
633	\end{proof}

869	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
870	  This is the classical Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement
871	  ``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete geometrically
872	  irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically closed field
873	  \(\bar k\)'', combined with the
874	  ``no-zeros-no-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' content of
875	  Stacks~\texttt{02P0}, in two ingredients:
876	
877	  \emph{Step (i) — algebraic Hartogs.} On the normal Noetherian scheme
878	  \(C\), a rational function regular at every codimension-\(1\) point
879	  extends uniquely to a global section of the structure sheaf
880	  (Stacks tag~\texttt{0BCK}: $\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) =
881	  \bigcap_{Q\in C^{(1)}} \mathcal O_{C, Q}$). On the smooth curve
882	  \(C\) every prime divisor \(Q\) is a codimension-\(1\) point and
883	  \(\ord_Q\) is the standard discrete valuation on
884	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\); the hypothesis \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every
885	  \(Q\) places \(f\) in the intersection
886	  \(\bigcap_Q \mathcal O_{C, Q} = \Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C)\).
887	
888	  \emph{Step (ii) — global sections are constants.} On a proper
889	  geometrically irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically
890	  closed field \(\bar k\), the global-sections ring of the structure
891	  sheaf coincides with the base field,
892	  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4; or
893	  cohomologically: \(\Gamma\) is finite-dimensional over \(\bar k\)
894	  by Hartshorne~III.5.2, hence integral over \(\bar k\), hence
895	  algebraic, hence \(\bar k\) by algebraic closure). Combining
896	  step~(i) with step~(ii) places \(f\) in the image of the constant
897	  inclusion \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\), as required.
898	
899	  \emph{Mathlib status.} At snapshot \texttt{b80f227} both ingredients
900	  are project-bespoke Mathlib gaps: the Lean substrate helper carries

902	  extraction makes the upstream dependency explicit for future
903	  iterations.

906	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
907	
908	\begin{corollary}
909	
910	
911	\leanok
912	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
913	single simple pole]
914	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
915	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
916	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
917	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
918	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
919	  
920	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
921	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
922	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
923	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
924	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
925	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
926	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
927	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
928	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
929	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
930	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
931	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
932	  \begin{itemize}
933	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
934	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
935	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
936	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
937	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
938	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
939	  \end{itemize}
940	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
941	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
942	\end{corollary}
943	
944	\begin{proof}
945	  
946	  
947	  
948	\leanok
949	  By
950	  REF,
951	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
952	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
953	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
954	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
955	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
956	  identification of
957	  REF), is a one-dimensional
958	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
959	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
960	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
961	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
962	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
963	
964	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
965	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
966	  REF the section
967	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
968	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
969	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
970	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
971	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
972	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
973	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
974	  of the statement are now immediate.
975	
976	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
977	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
978	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
979	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
980	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).

988	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
989	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
990	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
991	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	\label{chap:avr_for_rr}
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
10	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
11	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
12	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
13	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
14	% keystone directly). The headline prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
15	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
16	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
17	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
18	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
46	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) proper, \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and
47	base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\). Because \(0\) is a \emph{fixed point} of
48	scaling, the \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)\) is
49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\
50	\(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\). Specialising \(x = 1\) gives that \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant, and since

54	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
